title: Don’t hatch the messenger? On the desirability of restricting the political activity of bureaucrats
abstract: Many countries place restrictions on the political rights of government workers. This includes limitations on political activities such as taking an active part in political campaigns. Are such restrictions desirable? We present a formal welfare analysis of this question. Bureaucrats’ political activities affect voter perceptions of the government and this can have informational benefits. However, they can also induce policy mistakes and are susceptible to ‘noise’ from some bureaucrats’ innate desire for political expression. When politicians have limited control over bureaucrats and successfully coordinate with voters, bureaucrats’ political activities can be desirable. In most cases, however, banning political activities is optimal.
summary_en: Many countries restrict the political activity of civil servants (no campaigning, no party affiliation). Are such restrictions welfare-improving? This paper provides a formal welfare analysis. Bureaucrats' political activities can inform voters about the government — a benefit. But they can also induce policy mistakes and are susceptible to "noise" from bureaucrats' innate political preferences. Result: when politicians have limited control over bureaucrats and voters coordinate well, political activities can be desirable. In most cases, however, banning them is optimal. A theoretical foundation for common civil-service restrictions.
policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungarian civil-service legislation (BM, Ministry of Public Administration). The paper supports the general principle of restricting bureaucrats' political activity while acknowledging specific settings where activity could be beneficial. Useful framework for civil-service-reform debates. Caveat: theoretical; specific Hungarian institutional context (political-appointment structures, voter-information flows) would shape the concrete application.
